items.q: Why is ice-albedo feedback a positive feedback loop?
items.correct: Less ice means less reflective surface, so more sunlight is absorbed, causing more warming and more melting.
items.misconception: Because warming is positive (a good thing).
items.random_wrong: Because polar bears help spread heat.
items.why: Students confuse 'positive' (mathematics) with 'good' (everyday). Positive feedback amplifies the change, in any direction - a negative outcome can result from a positive feedback loop.

items.q: How does the Brundtland Report define sustainable development?
items.correct: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as 'no growth'. The Brundtland definition explicitly balances development AND environmental concerns - it's about INTERGENERATIONAL equity, not freezing change.

items.q: Why are invasive species often more harmful than native species?
items.correct: They lack natural predators or competitors in their new environment, so they spread unchecked.
items.misconception: They are inherently more aggressive than native species.
items.random_wrong: They are larger than native species.
items.why: Students attribute success to species traits. The key factor is the ABSENCE OF CONTROLS - the same species may be ordinary in its native range, where co-evolved predators and competitors keep it in check.
Correct answer: Soil pH. “Predators” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An organism's role in an ecosystem is its?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Niche. “Habitat” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many trophic levels does a typical food chain have at minimum?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 levels. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
